coredata.dc:title: A “Turn-On” thiol functionalized fluorescent carbon quantum dot based chemosensory system for arsenite detection
coredata.dc:creator: D., Pooja
Saini, Sonia
Thakur, Anupma
Kumar, Baban
Tyagi, Sachin
Nayak, Manoj K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged out as promising fluorescent probes for hazardous heavy metals detection in recent past. In this study, water soluble CQDs were synthesized by facile microwave pyrolysis of citric acid & cysteamine, and functionalized with ditheritheritol to impart thiol functionalities at surface for selective detection of toxic arsenite in water. Microscopic analysis reveals that the synthesized CQDs are of uniform size (diameter ∼5nm) and confirmed to have surface SH groups by FT-IR. The functionalized probe is then demonstrated for arsenite detection in water by “Turn-On” read out mechanism, which reduces the possibility of false positive signals associated with “turn off’ probes reported earlier. The blue luminescent functionalized CQDs exhibit increase in fluorescence intensity on arsenite addition in 5–100ppb wide detection range. The probe can be used for sensitive detection of arsenite in environmental water to a theoretical detection limit (3s) of 0.086ppb (R2 =0.9547) with good reproducibility at 2.6% relative standard deviation. The presented reliable, sensitive, rapid fCQDs probe demonstrated to exhibit high selectivity towards arsenite and exemplified for real water samples as well. The analytical performance of the presented probe is comparable to existing organic & semiconductor based optical probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arsenite
Carbon quantum dots
Turn–On
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis, functionalization and purification of CQDs
Analytical performance of fCQDs towards arsenite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pie chart of reported materails for optical detection of arsenite (Year 2010–2016).
Schematic of synthesis, functionalization of CQDs and proposed binding approach with arsenite.
(a) absorption and emission characteristics of CQDs and (b) effect of excitation wavelength on emission behavior of CQDs.
(a) HR-TEM micrograph of as synthesized CQDs (inset: SAED pattern) and (b) higher magnified micrograph of CQDs with lattice fringe information.
13C NMR analysis of as synthesized CQDs.
FT-IR spectra of as synthesized and functionalized CQDs.
Relative (a) Absorption and (b) emission charcteristcis of CQDs after functionalization.
(a) Effect of pH on CQDs & fCQDs performance and (b) sensing behvaiour of fCQDs towards arsenite at 10 and 100 ppb.
(a) LOD study (b) regression analysis (c) proposed best fit model and (d) Stern-Volmer plot for fCQDs behaviour towards 5–100ppb arsenite concentration.
Interfetence study of fCQDs towrads anions (a–b) and cations (c–d) at equal and 10 fold concentrations.
Functional groups observed in FT-IR spectra of CQDs and fCQDs.
Quantum yield parameters for QS, CQDs & fCQDs.
Real water samples analysis by fCQDs.
Comparative performance of earlier reported optical probes with presented fCQDs sensory probe.

head.ce:title: A “Turn-On” thiol functionalized fluorescent carbon quantum dot based chemosensory system for arsenite detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, New Delhi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Sectro-30 C, Chandigarh 160030, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, New Delhi, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged out as promising fluorescent probes for hazardous heavy metals detection in recent past. In this study, water soluble CQDs were synthesized by facile microwave pyrolysis of citric acid & cysteamine, and functionalized with ditheritheritol to impart thiol functionalities at surface for selective detection of toxic arsenite in water. Microscopic analysis reveals that the synthesized CQDs are of uniform size (diameter ∼5nm) and confirmed to have surface SH groups by FT-IR. The functionalized probe is then demonstrated for arsenite detection in water by “Turn-On” read out mechanism, which reduces the possibility of false positive signals associated with “turn off’ probes reported earlier. The blue luminescent functionalized CQDs exhibit increase in fluorescence intensity on arsenite addition in 5–100ppb wide detection range. The probe can be used for sensitive detection of arsenite in environmental water to a theoretical detection limit (3s) of 0.086ppb (R2 =0.9547) with good reproducibility at 2.6% relative standard deviation. The presented reliable, sensitive, rapid fCQDs probe demonstrated to exhibit high selectivity towards arsenite and exemplified for real water samples as well. The analytical performance of the presented probe is comparable to existing organic & semiconductor based optical probes.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic arsenic, which exists as arsenite (As(III)) and arsenate (As(V)), in water has intrigues lots of global concern due to its toxic and carcinogenic effects [1,2]. Amongst these states, arsenite is likely thought to be relatively more mobile and toxic over other inorganic and organic states. Owing to its complex formation ability with various enzymes/co-enzymes involved in human metabolism through AsO or AsS linkages [3–8] it leads to acute and chronic physiological health effects such as cancer, liver and kidney damage, pigmentation, acute renal failure, neuropathy, abdominal pain, cell dysfunction, etc. [9,10]. Therefore the acceptance level of arsenite in drinking water is laid down below 10ppb by World Health Organization (WHO) [11].

Due to well recognized importance of arsenite detection, plethora of conventional techniques including chromatography, mass spectrometry, atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy, etc., are reported for its determination, however, they have been limited to laboratory sphere in view of expensive & sophisticated instrumentation requirement, longer analysis time, tedious sample preparation, personnel training and poor stability limits [12]. Besides, voltammetric approach has been widely investigated for ppb level arsenic detection, wherein a range of bulk and modified working electrodes including, noble metals, metal oxides, carbon nanostructures, etc., have been employed [13–18]. On the other hand, fluorometric methods have also gained tremendous attentions for heavy metal/metalloid ions including arsenic detection owing to their simplicity, easy operation, tunability and fast response. Yet the sensitivity and selectivity characteristics of optical probe decides their fate for field translation. Over the years, semiconductor QDs (CdTe, CdS), conventional organic dyes, gold nanoparticles, gold nanoclusters, fluorescent polymers, organic fluorophores, etc., have been reported for optical detection of arsenite, as shown in Fig. 1 . [19]. Wang et al. reported glutathione capped CdTe QDs for ultra trace arsenic detection to 2×10−8 molL−1 level [20]. In an another work, Villiers and co-workers demonstrated glutathione capped CdSe/ZnS core–shell QDs for mobile arsenic(III) measurement in surface waters [21]. Recently, Ensafi et al. reported a simple and sensitive fluorimetric aptasensor for the ultrasensitive detection of arsenite based on cysteamine stabilized CdTe/ZnS QDs aggregation [22]. But, irrespective of excellent optical and chemical stability of semiconductor QDs over existing organic fluorophores for arsenite determination, their real world application realization is still a challenge due to their toxicity in terms of their heavy metals based composition. Further, their poor aqueous solubility, low quantum yield in water, presence of the surface trap sites and complex synthesis from expensive & toxic precursors remains a considerable challenge for researchers. In view of these facts, the development of an alternative environment friendly and cost effective probe for trace arsenite detection remains an insight of research for peer group.

Of late carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an emerging class of zero dimensional nanomaterials have been widely employed in the field of catalysis, bio-imaging, fuel cells, optoelectronics, etc., due to their superior chemical stability, low toxicity, tunable surface functionalities, high aqueous solubility and resistivity to photo-bleaching [23,24]. In recent past, they have been used for sensing of several cations, anions and organic molecules [25]. Liu and coworkers have reported a novel carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescent probe for fluoride detection [26]. Copper ions detection has been demonstrated by Dong and co-workers using polyamine functionalized CQDs [27]. Similarly, CQDs have been demonstrated for detection of mercury and silver ions [28,29]. These reports projects CQDs as leading and promising alternatives for the existing organic fluorescent markers and toxic semiconductor probes for optical sensing.

Therefore, fascinated by recent past application of CQDs in sensors and motivated by the need to design an alternative optical probe for arsenite detection, herein, we report dithiotheritol (DTT) functionalized CQDs for its detection in water by ‘turn-on’ mechanism, which is still not investigated to the best of our knowledge. The turn-on mechanism allows ease of measuring low concentration contrast relative to a dark background, which reduces the possibility of false positive signals. The presented probe utilize As-S bond (strength=379kJmol−1) chemistry for selective detection of arsenic. A vast number of sulfur containing ligands such as glutathione, dithioretiol, cysteine, mercapto-acetic acid, mercapto-propionic acid, etc., have been demonstrated for the arsenic detection in the recent past utilizing AsS bond strength in aqueous solution [23,36–38]. In our work, in view of available surface chemistry, we have used DTT, which possess free sulfhydryl groups, for functionalization on synthesized CQDs surface to selectively detect arsenite in water.

All chemicals procured were of analytical grade and used directly without any purification. CQDs were synthesized and functionalized using citric Acid [SRL, SISCO Research Laboratory)], cysteamine hydrochloride (Avra Synthesis) and dithiotheritol (DTT, Himedia). TRIS buffer (TRIS [tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane] powder, Fisher scientific) and arsenite stock solution (1000ppm) obtained from inorganic ventures were used to conduct all sensing experiments. Standard stock solutions (1000ppm) of various ions (NO3 −, NO2 −, Br−, PO4 2−, F− , Co2+, Ni2+, Li2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Rb2+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, As(V) and Se(IV)) purchased from Merck & Inorganic Ventures were used for interference study. Deionized water obtained from Millipore corporation grade water system (Milli-Q) was used to prepare all solutions and subsequent dilutions.

Microwave Oven (700W IFB MODEL=17PM-MEC1) was purchased from www.ifbappliances.com for the synthesis of CQDs in high power mode. Optical characteristics of synthesized CQDs were probed by UV–vis (Hitachi U-3010) and fluorescence (Varian Cary Eclipse) spectrophotometer. The fluorescence of dots was seen and photographed under UV cabinet (POPULAR INDIA). High resolution −Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Hitachi H-7500) was used to check the morphological and structural characteristics of CQDs. Incubator (INNOVA 4230, New Brunswick Scientific) was used for the functionalization of CQDs with DTT. Dialysis membrane (Spectrum Labs) was used for the purification of as synthesized and functionalized CQDs. NMR spectrum was recorded with 400MHz Avance-II spectrometer (Bruker Corporation). The functionalization of CQDs with DTT was confirmed by vibrational spectroscopic analysis with FT-IR (Varian 670 IR) and Raman spectroscopy (inVia RAMAN, RENISHAW).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, functionalization and purification of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized by microwave pyrolysis method. In a typical procedure, a mixture of citric acid (0.4202g) and cysteamine (2.101g) was diluted to 10mL with deionized water to obtain a transparent solution. The solution was stirred for 15min and subjected to microwave radiation for 3min in high power mode. The obtained brownish black powder was diluted to 30mL and stored in dark place at 4°C for further functionalization and characterization experiments. The as prepared nitrogen and sulfur embedded CQDs (10mL) were incubated overnight with DTT (10g) under stirring condition to obtain functionalized CQDs (fCQDs), which were further dialyzed against water for 12h to completely remove unreacted CQDs and unbound DTT ligand.

The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs and fCQDs was investigated by comparing their integrated intensities and the absorbance with reference dye, quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.01M H2SO4 (QY=54%). The quantum yield was measured using following equation:
Q
Y
S
= Q
Y R
×
η R
2
η S
2
×
I S
A S
×
A R
I S
Where, QY is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated photoluminescence intensity, A is the absorbance values/optical density, and η is refractive index. The subscript R & S refers to the reference fluorophore, QS, and sample, respectively. To measure QY, 10μL of as synthesized CQDs & fCQDs were diluted to 3mL in cuvettes before recording absorbance and emission values.

To nullify the effect of pH on the fluorescence of fCQDs, they were dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer (0.1M, pH=7) to carry out all sensing experiments. Briefly, 10μL of CQDs was successively diluted to 3mL with buffer and titrated with arsenite in 5–100ppb concentration range by standard addition method. The excitation wavelength was 280nm and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in 350–550nm scan range. The selectivity and sensitivity measurements were repeated thrice. For selectivity study, fCQDs probe was investigated against Co2+, Ni2+, Li2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Rb2+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, As(V) and Se(IV), NO3 −, NO2 −, Br−, PO4 2− and F− ions at 10ppb and 100ppb concentration prepared by diluting their 1000ppm stock solution.

The synthesis of CQDs involved dehydration between citric acid and cysteamine during microwave assisted pyrolysis to produce a fluorophore, which underwent intermolecular condensation, and self- assembled into CQDs through hydrophobic interaction and Π-Π stacking as shown in Fig. 2 . The synthesized CQDs exhibit thiol groups at its surface, which could be further used for fixing other ligands. During synthesis of intermediate “3”, citric acid and cysteamine underwent loss of three water molecules leading to surface passivation with nitrogen as well as sulfur groups to some extent (Fig. 2). Surface passivation facilitates effective radiative recombination and avoids surface exposure with different contaminants, which may lead to loss of optoelectronic properties [30]. Relative to semiconductor quantum dots, the preparation of CQDs is simple, straightforward and doesn’t require toxic precursors and organic solvents, which is advantageous. The as synthesized CQDs are then subjected to DTT functionalization using SS chemistry to impart SH functionalities on their surface. The additional −SH functionality were introduced intentionally in view of strong AsS chemistry. As(III) is known to form strong bonds with both O and S, where the bond strength of As-O (481kJmol−1) is slightly higher as compared to that of As-S (379kJmol−1). However, AsO bonds are highly susceptible to undergo hydrolysis under aqueous condition as compared AsS bonds [31]. Therefore As-S bond chemistry is preferred for design of arsenite selective sensor as it also facilitates selective removal of arsenic from body. The three sulphur groups are known to co-ordinate with As (III) species, as shown in Fig. 2, which is utilized as a possible mechanism for selective arsenite detection discussed in interference study section.

The UV–vis absorption and emission of as prepared CQDs is shown given in Fig. 3 (a), which clearly depicts a sharp peak at ∼350nm and small hump at ∼240nm. These observed characteristic peaks are ascribed n–π* transitions of the carbonyl and π–π* transition of the nitrogen heterocyclic sp2 domain, respectively [32]. When excited at 340nm, the centered emission of CQDs was observed at ∼412nm. The photograph of the dispersion under UV light exhibits a blue color (inset), further revealing that the resultant CQDs exhibit blue fluorescence. To further investigate the optical properties of the CQDs, a series of emission spectra of the CQDs were recorded at different excitation wavelengths in 250–350nm range, which featured the excitation wavelength independent behavior of synthesized CQDs [33]. In excitation range between 280 to 340nm, the emission behaviour of CQDs remained centered at 412nm as shown in Fig. 3(b). Whereas below, 280nm, they exhibited excitation dependent emission behavior [ESI, Fig. S1]. The observed complex PL emission behavior of CQDs could be associated with varied emission centers [34].

The particulate character of CQDs was observed by HR-TEM and is shown in Fig. 4 (a), which clearly depicts their spherical morphology with the diameter around 4–5nm and this is similar to that of C-dots as reported elsewhere [35]. SAED pattern reveals their poor crystalline nature, which may be attributed to the presence of nitrogen, sulphur and oxygen groups during pyrolysis [31]. This is further confirmed by the X-ray diffraction (data not shown) pattern of as synthesized CQDs. The lattice structure (d spacing, 0. 38nm) is discernible in the HR-TEM image, Fig. 4(b), and is higher than that of graphitic carbon interlayer spacing (0.33nm). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) result is in support with the HRTEM results [ESI, Fig. S2].

To obtain further structural insights about the CQDs, solid state 13C NMR spectrum (DMSO-d6, 100MHz,ppm) was characterized and is shown in Fig. 5 . The presence of peaks at 28.0ppm and 160.6ppm are assigned to methylene carbon with sulfur and amide carbon, respectively. The peaks in between 118.40–149.5ppm are attributed to carbon of aromatic pyridine ring, which illustrates that the structure has common with a CQDs as carbon-aromatic peaks are practically present in carbon dots [36]. The peak in between 71 and 72ppm associated with O-bonded aliphatic carbon and carboxyl carbons (169.05–174.54ppm), confirms presence of CO and O CO species in as synthesized CQDs. The FT-IR spectrum, Fig. 6 , is in line with proposed molecular structure. The bands at 1763cm−1 is due to CO bending vibrations of the carboxylic group, the bands at 732cm−1, 3372cm−1, 2029cm−1 and 2546cm−1 corresponds to CS stretching, NH bending, CC bending, and S-H stretching vibrations confirming presence of CS, CC, CO, CN specie in the sample along with presence of thiol moieties, which were utilized to further functionalize these CQDs with DTT to create thiol rich surface for selectivity towards. The DTT is attached to these CQDs via S-S interaction (226kJ bond energy) [37]. The presence of vibrational peak at 628cm−1 associated with S-S stretch and prominent free sulfhydryl (SH) peak at 2582cm−1confirms the functionalization of CQDs with DTT. Table 1 enlist these functional groups observed on CQDs and fCQDs surface supporting synthesis & functionalization of CQDs. Raman analysis of CQDs and fCQDs confirms their sp3 bonded structure and presence of aromatized species in obtained framework [ESI, Fig. S3]. The absorption and emission characteristics of CQDs after functionalization were also studied and are shown in Fig. 7 (a) & (b). It can be seen clearly that no effect in peak positioning is observed upon functionalization with thiol ligand. The emission characteristics of fCQDs at different excitation wavelength also showed excitation independent emission behavior, which indicate that DTT has only imparted surface functionality to CQDs and didn’t affect its internal structure [ESI, Fig. S4]. The quantum yield [ESI, Fig. S5] of as synthesized CQDs and fCQDs at 340nm under optimal parameters was measured to be ∼28% and ∼22%, respectively, by calibrating against quinine sulphate and presented in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of fCQDs towards arsenite
ce:section.ce:para:
To characterize analytical performance of fCQDs towards arsenite, it was firstly investigated in different pH environments (Fig. 8 (a)). It was observed that the probe is strongly sensitive to pH. Such behavior are alike to CQDs containing hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties [38]. Therefore to nullify the environmental samples pH effect, sensing experiments were performed in Tris buffer (pH=7). Fig. 8(b) shows the sensing behavior of fCQDs in absence and presence of arsenite (at 10ppb and 100ppb As(III) solution) under optimized conditions. It can be seen clearly that on addition of arsenite, significant increase in PL intensity is observed, attributed to “turn-on” optical probes. Most literature however reports fluorescence quenching as the readout mechanism for the sensor response towards arsenite. The turn-on mechanism on the other hand allows ease of measuring low concentration contrast relative to a dark background, which reduces the possibility of false positive signals & increases overall sensitivity. The mechanism for understanding this observed behavior could be attributed to binding of arsenite species with sulfur groups of fCQDs through SH of DTT ligand, which might have affected the electronic properties of CQDs. However, detailed investigation using ultrafast spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is required to unravel underlying mechanism. The association constant of the complex formation between fCQDs and As (III) was calculated using the fluorescence enhancement data with Benesi-Hildebrand method:
1
(
F O
− F )
=
1
(
F O
−
F b
)
+
1
K
(
F O
−
F b
)
[
A s
(
I I I
)
]
where, K is the apparent association constant, Fo & Fb is the fluorescence intensity of fCQDs in absence and presence of excess As(III)), and F is the observed fluorescence intensity of fCQDs in the presence of varied amounts of As(III). The value of K was obtained from 1/(F0 −F) vs 1/[As(III)] plot, where K is equal to the intercept/slope and is found to be 1.81×10 5 M−1[ESI, Fig. S6]. In comparison, as synthesized CQDs didn’t show a significant sensing performance [ESI, Fig. S7], as observed in case of fCQDs, which could be attributed to availability of more arsenite selective chelating groups (SH) due to DTT immobilization. The structure of the surface bound DTT-CQDs complex could be readily transformed into more stable As(III)-DTT-CQDs in the presence of As(III), leading to the shutdown of the non-radiative recombination pathway. Compared with other nanoparticle based optical sensors, this approach opens up various avenues for arsenite detection in environmental samples as well as other matrices. The existing probes are mainly metal-based semiconductor QDs (e.g. CdTe, CdS, ZnS, etc.), which have raised serious environmental and health concerns due to their elemental composition and toxicity issues [21,22]. Therefore, CQDs based optical probes are one of best alternatives to these for real world applications.

The observed interesting “turn-on” phenomenon further impelled us to investigate the fCQDs performance towards varied concentrations (5–100ppb) of arsenite and is shown in Fig. 9 (a) & (b). For sensing experiments different concentration of As(III) were incubated with optimized concentration of fCQDs for few seconds and then recorded in PL system. It can be seen clearly that a gradual increase in intensity occurred on addition of increasing amount of arsenic (III), which showed the detection capability of probe in wide detection range for 10μL fCQDs. The probe can be further extended to higher as well as lower concentration of arsenite subjected to fCQDs volume optimization. The regression curve exhibit high correlation coefficient (R2 =0.9873) for the complete concentration range studied with best fit linear equation. However, a model (Fig. 9(c)) is proposed to fit observed actual behaviour in 5–100ppb concentration range and fits best onto the triplicate runs towards arsenite sensing. Furthermore, predicted values are nearly in line with the experimental results obtained for respective arsenite concentration. The proposed model equation is:
y = 1.6
x 3
− 12
x 2
+ 48 x − 37
where, y is arsenite concentration and x is F/Fo. The predicted values of concentration using the proposed equation was validated with various experimental values and RMSE is calculated to be 1.7077 [ESI, Table 1]. The theoretical detection limit was calculated at a signal to noise ratio of 3 and fitting parameter of 0.9547 in 10–50ppb linear concentration range using Stern-Volmer equation and shown in Fig. 9(d):
I O
I
=
K
S V
( Q )
+ C
where, I0 and I are the luminescence intensity in the absence and presence of arsenite, respectively, KSV is the Stern−Volmer constant, Q is analyte concentration, and C is a constant close to 1. The calibration curve was almost linear in 10–50ppb range for a linear regression equation of I0/I=7.08×105C+0.7143 (R2 =0.9547) with precision of replicate measurements, expressed as a relative standard deviation (RSD) as 2.6% (n=3). The Ksv under above parameters was found to be 7.08×105 M−1 , which is relatively higher. The limit of detection, defined as the analyte concentration equivalent to 3s (s for standard deviation), & limit of quantification, equivalent to 10s, and was calculated to be 0.086ppb and 0.283ppb respectively. The precision of experiments for triplicates was calculated as a percentage using the ratio of standard deviation and means of replicates and is found to be 0.01%.

To determine the selectivity of the fCQDs probe, which is one of the most important criterion for a good metal ion sensor, interference experiments were carried out against 19 other kind of ions including NO3 −, NO2 −, Br−, PO4 2−, F−, Co2+, Ni2+, Li2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Rb2+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, As(V) and Se(IV) at equal and 10 fold concentrations. The effect of other inorganic form of arsenic was also studied in these concentrations. Fig. 10 (a)–(d) illustrates the PL response of the presented optical sensor in the presence of comparative anions and cations. Interestingly, none or negligible quenching is observed in presence of commonly interfering anions in arsenite sensing at lower concentration (10 ppb), which otherwise is significant, observed as “turn-on”, in case of arsenite, indicating insulation of fCQDs towards these anions. It shows the importance and practicality of the presented probe to discriminate arsenite amongst other anions at WHO recommended level. Similar behaviour was observed at even 10 fold concentrations (Fig. 10(b)). Likewise anions, the cations were also not observed to “turn on” the probe to significant level. The commonly existing another form of arsenic, i.e. arsenate also didn’t affect PL intensity of fCQDs probe by similar mechanism. However a decrease in PL intensity was observed to a little extent. Thus, the probe exhibits high selectivity toward arsenite. Whilst, to test the practical applicability of the presented optical probe for detecting As(III), competitive ion study experiments were also carried out [ESI, Fig. S8], which further manifests the high selectivity of probe towards arsenite by means of “turn on” read out sensing mechanism. The observed high selectivity of fCQDs could be understood in terms of strong AsS bond strength, which is also responsible to facilitate its selective removal from body [35]. Further the high value of association constant between fCQDs-As(III) complex may be the possible reason for observed selective behaviour.

In consideration of high sensitivity and selectivity of fCQDs probes, their practicality was validated against real water samples collected from lake, ground and well of various districts of Punjab, India. Before analysis, the collected water samples were filtered through a 0.22μm membrane to remove suspended particles and spiked with arsenite standard solution to achieve 10ppb arsenite concentration. It was observed that fluorescence intensity is enhanced on addition of 10ppb spiked water samples collected from various sources and the accuracy of the fCQDs was determined by%recovery calculated with predicted values as shown in Table 3 . The results showed that analytical recoveries of arsenite added to water samples were ranged from 96% to 102%. This implies that despite of the presence of numerous minerals in real water samples, the proposed sensing platform can effectively distinguish between arsenite contaminated and non-contaminated water samples to even 10ppb level, satisfying the WHO (10ppb) & USEPA (50ppb) criterion of safe limit in drinking water. Further, there was no significant difference between the values determined by our approach and the certified one, which reveal that the label free CQDs based probe can be utilized for arsenite determination in natural water samples. Overall, in comparison with some representative nanoparticles based optical probes [20–22,39–42], as shown in Table 4 , our label free “turn-on” based PL assay utilizing “carbon based” environmental friendly probe, is relatively simple, cost-effective, sensitive and selective.

In summary, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence based chemo sensory system toward arsenite in water is reported based on analyte induced “turn-on” mechanism. The environmentally friendly carbon based optical probes offer a substitution over existing toxic semiconductor ones. These probes are surface tuned with arsenite selective SH groups to selectively detect As(III) in water samples in wide detection range and exhibit a theoretical detection limit of 0.086ppb, which is well below the WHO recommended value. This simple, cost-effective, highly sensitive and selective probe allows the detection of arsenite in natural water samples. These findings concretes a route for intervention of highly fluorescent CQDs in optical detection of arsenite by less commonly observed “turn-on” mechanism, which requires further understanding of underlying physical phenomenon.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support received from Director, CSIO to carry out this work. Financial support received from MLP0025 (Translational Research Project) is also acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2017.01.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Arsenic intake via water and food by a population living in an arsenic-affected area of Bangladesh
A market basket survey of inorganic arsenic in food
Identification of complexes containing glutathione with As (III), Sb (III) Cd (II), Hg (II), Tl (I), Pb (II) or Bi (III) by electrospray ionization mass spectrometry
Synergy of glutathione, dithiothreitol and N-acetyl-l-cysteine self-assembled monolayers for electrochemical assay: sensitive determination of arsenic (III) in environmental and drinking water
Arsenite and arsenate binding to dissolved humic acids: influence of pH, type of humic acid, and aluminum
Electrochemical determination of arsenite using a gold nanoparticle modified glassy carbon electrode and flow analysis
Water analysis: emerging contaminants and current issues
Chronic exposure of arsenic via drinking water and its adverse health impacts on humans
Arsenic: toxicity, oxidative stress and human disease
Effect of natural organic matter on arsenic release from soils and sediments into groundwater
Speciation and detection of arsenic in aqueous samples: a review of recent progress in non-atomic spectrometric methods
Co-electrodeposited rGO/MnO 2 nanohybrid for arsenite detection in water by stripping voltammetry
Electrochemical determination of arsenic (III) with ultra-high anti-interference performance using Au–Cu bimetallic nanoparticles
Selective electrochemical sensing for arsenite using rGO/Fe3O4 nanocomposites
Electrochemical detection of trace arsenic (III) by nanocomposite of nanorod-like α-MnO2 decorated with ∼5nm Au nanoparticles: considering the change of arsenic speciation
Electrochemical determination of inorganic mercury and arsenic—a review
Dealing with interference challenge in the electrochemical detection of As (III)—a complexometric masking approach
